## Strategy context

Route A.2.b — gated on A.2.a (`Picard_FlatteningStratification.tex`, written this iter) + A.1.a (`Picard_RelativeSpec.tex`, on disk). ~1500-2500 LOC, ~10-17 iters. **Load-bearing FGA piece** — band widened iter-174 per strategy-critic (Mathlib has no Grassmannian scheme).

This builds the Quot scheme: for a projective scheme `X/S` and a coherent sheaf `F` on `X`, the Quot functor parameterizes quotients of `F` with given Hilbert polynomial. Per Nitsure §5.

The output is consumed by A.2.c (FGA Pic representability assembly).

## Required content

### Definition: Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf
For `f : X → S` projective with `S` Noetherian and `F` coherent on `X`, the Hilbert polynomial `P_F(n) := χ(F ⊗ O_X(n))` (Euler characteristic at twist `n`). State the standard polynomial-eventually property (Hartshorne III.5.2). Cite from `references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf`.

### Definition: Quot functor
For a projective morphism `X/S` with a coherent sheaf `F` on `X` and a polynomial `P`, the functor
$$\mathrm{Quot}^P_{F/X/S} : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathbf{Set}$$
sends `T → S` to the set of equivalence classes of quotient sheaves `f_T^*F ↠ Q` where `Q` is coherent on `X_T`, `T`-flat, and the Hilbert polynomial of `Q` (over each fiber) is `P`.

### Theorem: Quot scheme representability
Statement (Nitsure §5, Theorem 5): the functor `Quot^P_{F/X/S}` is representable by a projective `S`-scheme `Quot^P_{F/X/S}`.

### Proof outline (Nitsure §5 verbatim, then project notation)

Quote Nitsure §5 verbatim. Then split into sub-steps:

1. **Boundedness**. Use cohomology-and-base-change (Stacks 02KH) to reduce to the case where all quotients `f_T^*F ↠ Q` are uniformly bounded.

2. **Grassmannian embedding**. Pick `m` large enough that twisting `F(m)` makes the Quot functor an open subfunctor of the Grassmannian `Gr_d(H^0(F(m)))` for appropriate `d`. **This requires a Grassmannian scheme — absent from Mathlib at the pinned commit, so a project-side sub-build is needed.**

3. **Locus of flat quotients is open in Grassmannian**. Use the flattening stratification (A.2.a) to identify the locus where the universal quotient is `T`-flat; this is a finite union of strata, hence a locally-closed subscheme of the Grassmannian. The open part of this stratification gives the desired open subfunctor.

4. **Closed in projective Grassmannian**. The flatness condition is closed, hence `Quot^P` is closed in the Grassmannian, hence projective.

### Sub-lemmas

- **`def:hilbert_polynomial`** — Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf.
- **`def:quot_functor`** — the Quot functor as `(Sch/S)^op → Set`.
- **`def:grassmannian_scheme`** — the Grassmannian `Gr_d(V)` for a finite-rank free `O_S`-module `V`. **NEW sub-build, Mathlib gap.**
- **`thm:grassmannian_representable`** — Grassmannian is representable.
- **`thm:quot_representable`** — main statement.

### Lean signature targets

- `def:hilbert_polynomial` → `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial`
- `def:quot_functor` → `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor`
- `def:grassmannian_scheme` → `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian`
- `thm:quot_representable` → `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotScheme`

## Required citations

Read verbatim from:
- `references/nitsure-hilbert-quot.pdf` §5 (Quot scheme construction).
- `references/nitsure-hilbert-quot.pdf` §4 (Grassmannian, if introduced separately).
- `references/stacks-coherent.md` for cohomology-and-base-change (Stacks 02KH).

## Out of scope

- Do NOT write the Lean file.
- Do NOT touch `content.tex`.
- Do NOT re-prove flattening stratification — `\uses{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}`.
- Do NOT scope into Hilbert scheme separately (the Quot scheme subsumes Hilbert for `F = O_X`).

`\input{...}` is the plan agent's job.

Standard. Flag the Grassmannian scheme as a sub-prerequisite: it might warrant its own chapter `Picard_GrassmannianScheme.tex` (writer iter-175+). If you decide that splitting Grassmannian into a separate chapter is the cleaner organisation, note it under "Notes for Plan Agent".

103	  \label{thm:some_label}
104	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}

109	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
110	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."

127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.

135	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137

7	% relative-spectrum functor \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op}
8	% \to \mathrm{Sch}/X used by the relative Picard functor on a product C \times_k T.

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of $J = \Pic^0_{C/k}$ as a $g$-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	$\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)$
20	on the category of $k$-schemes. Representing $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}$ requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve $C \times_k T \to T$, which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebras
23	$\mathcal{A}$, the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	$\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X$. This chapter packages the construction
25	of $\underline{\Spec}_X(-)$ and its three core properties --- universal property, affine
26	base, base change --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of $J$ (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
35	
36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here $S$ is a scheme, and $\mathcal{A}$ is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebra. This means that $\mathcal{A}$ is a
49	  %  sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  $\mathcal{O}_S$-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let $X$ be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf $\mathcal{A}$ of $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebras (i.e. an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
54	  equipped with $\mathcal{O}_X$-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  $\mathcal{O}_X$-module is quasi-coherent. We write $\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)$ for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebras and
58	  $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}
60	
61	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
62	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
63	
64	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open $U = \Spec R \subseteq X$ one
65	sets $\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))$ regarded as an $R$-algebra, and the local pieces
66	glue compatibly because $\mathcal{A}$ is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
67	$\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)$ for an inclusion
68	$V = \Spec S \subseteq U$ gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).
69	
70	\begin{theorem}\leanok
71	[Relative spectrum exists]
72	  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
73	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}
74	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
75	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec)
76	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).
77	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
78	  % "In Situation \ref{situation-relative-spec}.
79	  %  There exists a morphism of schemes
80	  %  $$
81	  %  \pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
82	  %  $$
83	  %  with the following properties:
84	  %  \begin{enumerate}
85	  %  \item for every affine open $U \subset S$ there exists an isomorphism
86	  %  $i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))$ over $U$, and
87	  %  \item for $U \subset U' \subset S$ affine open the composition
88	  %  $$
89	  %  \xymatrix{
90	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \ar[r]^{i_U^{-1}} &
91	  %  \pi^{-1}(U) \ar[rr]^{inclusion} & &
92	  %  \pi^{-1}(U') \ar[r]^{i_{U'}} &
93	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))
94	  %  }
95	  %  $$
96	  %  is the open immersion of Lemma \ref{lemma-spec-inclusion} above.
97	  %  \end{enumerate}
98	  %  Moreover, $\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})$
99	  %  is unique up to unique isomorphism over $S$."
100	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
101	  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
102	  Let $X$ be a scheme and $\mathcal{A}$ a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebra
103	  (Definition~\ref{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}). There exists a scheme
104	  $\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$ together with an affine morphism
105	  $\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X$ such that
106	  \begin{enumerate}
107	    \item for every affine open $U \subseteq X$, there is an isomorphism
108	      $i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))$ over $U$;
109	    \item for $U \subseteq U' \subseteq X$ both affine open, the composite
110	      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
111	      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
112	      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
113	      $\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)$.
114	  \end{enumerate}
115	  The pair $(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}), \pi)$ is unique up to unique isomorphism
116	  over $X$.
117	\end{theorem}
118	
119	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Follows immediately from
120	% Lemmas \ref{lemma-relative-glueing},
121	% \ref{lemma-spec-inclusion}, and
122	% \ref{lemma-transitive-spec}.
123	% Uniqueness is stated in the last sentence of
124	% Lemma \ref{lemma-relative-glueing}."
125	\begin{proof}
126	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
127	  \leanok
128	  By the open-cover gluing principle for schemes (Mathlib
129	  \texttt{Scheme.GlueData} / \texttt{AffineScheme.glueOpens}) it suffices to specify
130	  affine local pieces $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))$ for each affine open $U \subseteq X$ and
131	  open-immersion transition data
132	  $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \hookrightarrow \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$ for $U \subseteq U'$
133	  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps
134	  $\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)$; quasi-coherence yields the cartesian square
135	  $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$, so each transition is an
136	  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
137	  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.
138	\end{proof}

141	[Universal property of the relative spectrum]
142	  \label{thm:relative_spec_univ}
143	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.UniversalProperty}

176	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-spec + section-spec
177	  functor description); [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(b).}
178	  Let $X$ be a scheme and $\mathcal{A}$ a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebra.
179	  Then $\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$ represents the functor
180	  $F : \Sch^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}$,
181	  $T \mapsto F(T) := \{(f, \varphi) : f : T \to X \text{ a morphism},\;

195	% (proof of lemma-spec, L553-L600, ~50 lines covering the Zariski sheaf property,

200	\begin{proof}
201	  \uses{thm:relative_spec_exists, thm:relative_spec_affine_base, thm:relative_spec_base_change}
202	  Following Stacks tag 01LQ (proof of lemma-spec): the functor $F$ is a Zariski sheaf
203	  because morphisms of schemes and $\mathcal{O}$-algebra maps both glue under open covers.
204	  Choose an affine open cover $X = \bigcup U_i$. The subfunctor
205	  $F_i \subseteq F$ of pairs $(f, \varphi)$ with $f(T) \subseteq U_i$ is representable by
206	  $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U_i))$ via the affine-base case
207	  (Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_affine_base}) together with the
208	  base-change identification $F_i \cong F_{U_i,\,\mathcal{A}|_{U_i}}$
209	  (Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_base_change}). Each $F_i \hookrightarrow F$ is an open
210	  subfunctor (pull back $U_i$ along $f$). The $F_i$ jointly cover $F$ since the $U_i$
211	  cover $X$. The gluing of representable open subfunctors produces a representing scheme,
212	  which is canonically isomorphic to the glued scheme $\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$
213	  of Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_exists}.
214	\end{proof}

437	\section{Lean encoding}
438	\label{sec:relspec_lean_encoding}
439	
440	The Lean target file is the new \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}.
441	The construction proceeds by gluing absolute affine pieces: for each affine open
442	$U \subseteq X$ with $U = \Spec R$ and $A := \mathcal{A}(U)$ regarded as an
443	$R$-algebra, the local piece is $\Spec A$ with structure morphism $\Spec A \to \Spec R = U$
444	induced by the algebra unit $R \to A$. Mathlib provides \texttt{IsAffineOpen.toScheme} for
445	the affine-open inclusion and the absolute \texttt{Scheme.Spec} functor for the local
446	pieces; the transition isomorphisms come from quasi-coherence of $\mathcal{A}$ via
447	\texttt{QuasiCoherent.tensorIso} (or the explicit
448	$\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)$ for $V = \Spec S \subseteq U$).
449	The gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData} (general construction) or
450	the convenience wrapper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme.glueOpens}; the cocycle
451	condition reduces to Stacks \texttt{lemma-transitive-spec}. The universal property
452	(Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_univ}) is expressed as a
453	\texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} witness, providing the natural bijection
454	$\Hom_X(T, \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})) \cong
455	\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_*\mathcal{O}_T)$ as a
456	\texttt{Yoneda}-style structure. The base-change isomorphism
457	(Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_base_change}) and functoriality
458	(Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_functorial}) are derived from the universal property by
459	Yoneda. No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a definitional/structural
460	construction on top of existing Mathlib affine-scheme infrastructure.

287	\section{The Hilbert and Quot Functors}
288	
289	
290	
291	
292	
293	\centerline{\large\bf The Functors $\Hilb_{\PP^n}$}
294	
295	
296	\medskip
297	
298	The main problem addressed in this series of lectures, in its simplest
299	form, is as follows. If $S$ is a locally noetherian scheme, 
300	a \textbf{family of subschemes of $\PP^n$ parametrised by $S$} will mean
301	a closed subscheme $Y \subset \PP^n_S = \PP^n_{\Z}\times S$ 
302	such that $Y$ is flat over $S$. 
303	If $f: T\to S$ is any morphism of locally noetherian schemes, then
304	by pull-back we get a family $f^*(Y) = (\id \times f)^{-1}(Y) 
305	\subset \PP^n_T$ 
306	parametrised by $T$, from a family $Y$ parametrised by $S$.
307	This defines a contravariant functor $\Hilb_{\PP^n}$ 
308	from the category of all locally noetherian schemes to the category of sets, 
309	which associates to any $S$ the set of all such families
310	$$\Hilb_{\PP^n}(S) = \{ Y \subset \PP^n_S \,|\,
311	Y \mbox{ is flat over }S \}$$
312	
313	\textbf{Question:} Is the functor $\Hilb_{\PP^n}$ representable?
314	
315	Grothendieck proved that this question has an affirmative answer, that is, 
316	there exists a locally noetherian scheme $\hilb_{\PP^n}$ together with
317	a family $Z \subset \PP^n_{\Z}\times \hilb_{\PP^n}$ 
318	parametrised by $\hilb_{\PP^n}$,
319	such that any family $Y$ over $S$ is obtained as the
320	pull-back of $Z$ 
321	by a uniquely determined morphism $\varphi_Y : S \to \hilb_{\PP^n}$.
322	In other words, $\Hilb_{\PP^n}$ is isomorphic to 
323	the functor $Mor(-,\hilb_{\PP^n})$.
324	
325	
326	
327	
328	
329	\medskip
330	
331	
332	\bigskip
333	
334	
335	\centerline{\large\bf The Functors $\Quot_{\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}}$}
336	\nopagebreak
337	\medskip
338	\nopagebreak
339	A family $Y$ of subschemes of $\PP^n$ parametrised by $S$
340	is the same as 
341	a coherent quotient sheaf $q: {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n_S} \to {\mathcal O}_Y$ on 
342	$\PP^n_S$, such that ${\mathcal O}_Y$ is flat over $S$. 
343	This way of looking at the functor $\Hilb_{\PP^n}$
344	has the following fruitful generalisation. 
345	
346	
347	Let $r$ be any positive integer. 
348	A \textbf{family of quotients of $\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}$ 
349	parametrised by a locally noetherian scheme $S$} will mean 
350	a pair $(\F,q)$ consisting of 
351	
352	(i) a coherent sheaf $\F$ on $\PP^n_S$ which is flat over $S$, and
353	
354	(ii) a surjective ${\mathcal O}_{\PP^n_S}$-linear homomorphism of sheaves 
355	$q:\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n_S}  \to \F$.
356	
357	Two such families $(\F,q)$ and $(\F,q)$ parametrised by $S$ will 
358	be regarded as equivalent if there exists an isomorphism $f: \F \to \F'$
359	which takes $q$ to $q'$, that is, the following diagram commutes.
360	$$\begin{array}{ccc}
361	\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n} &\stackrel{q}{\to} & \F \\
362	\|                  &     & ~ \dna {\scriptstyle f} \\
363	\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n} &\stackrel{q'}{\to} & \F' 
364	\end{array}$$
365	This is the same as the condition 
366	$\ker(q) = \ker(q')$. We will denote by $\langle \F, q \rangle$ 
367	an equivalence class. If $f : T\to S$ is a morphism of 
368	locally noetherian schemes, then pulling back the quotient
369	$q: \oplus^r{\mathcal O}_{\PP^n_S} \to \F$ 
370	under $\id\times f : \PP^n_T \to \PP^n_S$
371	defines a family $f^*(q) : \oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n_T} \to f^*(\F)$ over $T$, 
372	which makes sense as tensor product is right-exact and preserves flatness. 
373	The operation of pulling back respects equivalence of families,
374	therefore it gives rise to a contravariant functor
375	$\Quot_{\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}}$ from the category of
376	all locally noetherian schemes to the category of sets, 
377	by putting 
378	$$\Quot_{\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}}(S) = 
379	\{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }S\}$$
380	
381	
382	It is immediate that the functor $\Quot_{\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}}$
383	satisfies faithfully flat descent. It was proved by
384	Grothendieck that in fact the above functor
385	is representable on the category of all locally noetherian schemes 
386	by a scheme $\quot_{\oplus^r{\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}}$. 
387	
388	
389	\medskip
390	
391	
392	\bigskip
393	
394	\centerline{\large\bf The Functors $\Hilb_{X/S}$ and $\Quot_{E/X/S}$}
395	
396	\medskip
397	
398	The above functors $\Hilb_{\PP^n}$ and $\Quot_{\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}}$
399	admit the following simple generalisations. 
400	Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme and let $X\to S$ be a finite type
401	scheme over it. Let $E$ be a coherent sheaf on $X$. 
402	Let $Sch_S$ denote the category of all locally noetherian schemes
403	over $S$. For any $T\to S$ in $Sch_S$, a \textbf{family of quotients of $E$ 
404	parametrised by $T$} will mean 
405	a pair $(\F,q)$ consisting of 
406	
407	(i) a coherent sheaf $\F$ on $X_T = X\times_ST$ 
408	such that the schematic support of $\F$ is proper over $T$ 
409	and $\F$ is flat over $T$, together with 
410	
411	(ii) a surjective ${\mathcal O}_{X_T}$-linear homomorphism of sheaves 
412	$q:E_T  \to \F$ where $E_T$ is the pull-back of $E$ under
413	the projection $X_T \to X$.
414	
415	Two such families $(\F,q)$ and $(\F,q)$ parametrised by $T$ will 
416	be regarded as equivalent if $\ker(q) = \ker(q')$), 
417	and $\langle \F, q \rangle$ will denote 
418	an equivalence class. Then as properness and flatness are preserved
419	by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact, 
420	the pull-back of $\langle \F, q \rangle$ under an $S$-morphism $T'\to T$ is
421	well-defined, which gives
422	a set-valued contravariant functor 
423	$\Quot_{E/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets$ under which   
424	$$T\mapsto  \{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }T\}$$
425	
426	When $E = {\mathcal O}_X$, the functor $\Quot_{{\mathcal O}_X/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets$ 
427	associates to $T$ the set of all closed subschemes $Y\subset X_T$
428	that are proper and flat over $T$. We denote this functor by $\Hilb_{X/S}$.
429	
430	Note in particular that we have
431	$$\Hilb_{\PP^n} = \Hilb_{\PP^n_{\Z}/\spec \Z} \mbox{ and }
432	\Quot_{\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}} = 
433	\Quot_{\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n_{\Z}}/\PP^n_{\Z}/\spec \Z}$$ 
434	
435	
436	It is clear that the functors $\Quot_{E/X/S}$ and $\Hilb_{X/S}$
437	satisfy faithfully flat descent, 
438	so it makes sense to pose the question of their
439	representability. 
440

446	\bigskip
447	
448	\centerline{\large\bf Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials}
449	
450	\medskip
451	
452	
453	Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
454	a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
455	Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on 
456	$X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
457	the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
458	the function 
459	$${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) = 
460	\sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
461	where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the 
462	coherence and properness conditions. The fact that $\chi(F(m))$
463	is indeed a polynomial in $m$ under the above assumption is a special
464	case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
465	
466	
467	
468	Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
469	and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
470	be any coherent sheaf on 
471	$X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
472	we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$ 
473	which is the Hilbert polynomial of
474	the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
475	calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$. 
476	If $\F$ is flat over $S$ then the function $s\mapsto {\Phi}_s$ 
477	from the set of points of $S$ to the polynomial ring $\Q[\lambda]$ is 
478	known to be locally constant on $S$. 
479	
480	
481	This shows that the functor $\Quot_{E/X/S}$ naturally decomposes
482	as a co-product 
483	$$\Quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$$
484	where for any polynomial ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$, the functor 
485	$\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ associates
486	to any $T$ the set of all equivalence classes of 
487	families $\langle \F,q \rangle$ such that at each $t\in T$ the 
488	Hilbert polynomial of the restriction $\F_t$, calculated
489	using the pull-back of $L$, is ${\Phi}$. Correspondingly,
490	the representing scheme  $\quot_{E/X/S}$, when it exists, naturally decomposes
491	as a co-product
492	$$\quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$$
493	
494	\textbf{Note } 
495	We will generally take $X$ to be (quasi-)projective over $S$,
496	and $L$ to be a relatively very ample line bundle. Then indeed 
497	the Hilbert and Quot functors are representable by schemes,
498	but not in general.
499

626	\textbf{(3) } \textbf{Grassmannian as a Hilbert scheme }
627	Let $\Phi = 1 \in \Q[\lambda]$. Then the Hilbert scheme
628	$\hilb_{\PP^n}^{1,{\mathcal O}(1)}$ is $\PP^n_{\Z}$ itself.
629	More generally, let $\Phi_r = {r + \lambda \choose r}\in \Q[\lambda]$
630	where $r \ge 0$. 
631	The Hilbert scheme $\hilb_{\PP^n}^{\Phi_r,{\mathcal O}(1)}$ is isomorphic to the 
632	Grassmannian scheme $\grass(n+1, r+1)$ over $\Z$. 
633	This can be seen via the following steps, whose detailed verification 
634	is left to the reader as an exercise.
635	
636	(i) The Grassmannian scheme 
637	$\grass(n+1, r+1)$ over $\Z$ parametrises a tautological family of 
638	subschemes of $\PP^n$ with Hilbert polynomial $\Phi_r$. 
639	Therefore we get a natural transformation 
640	$h_{\grass(n+1, r+1)} \to \Hilb_{\PP^n}^{\Phi_r,{\mathcal O}(1)}$.
641	
642	
643	(ii) Any closed subscheme $Y \subset \PP^n_k$
644	with Hilbert polynomial ${\Phi}_r$, where $k$ is any field, 
645	is isomorphic to $\PP^r_k$ embedded linearly in $\PP^n_k$ over $k$.
646	If $V$ is a vector bundle over a noetherian base $S$, and if
647	$Y\subset \PP(V)$ is a closed subscheme flat over $S$ with each
648	schematic fiber $Y_s$ an $r$-dimensional 
649	linear subspace of the projective space
650	$\PP(V_s)$, then $Y$ is defines
651	a rank $r+1$ quotient vector bundle 
652	$V = \pi_*{\mathcal O}_{\PP(V)}(1) \to \pi_*{\mathcal O}_Y(1)$  
653	where $\pi : \PP(V) \to S$ denotes the projection. 
654	This gives a natural transformation
655	$\Hilb_{\PP^n}^{{\Phi}_r,{\mathcal O}(1)} \to h_{\grass(n+1, r+1)}$.
656	
657	(iii) The above two natural transformations are inverses of each other.

773	\centerline{\large\bf Construction of Grassmannian}
774	
775	
776	\medskip
777	
778	The following explicit construction of 
779	the Grassmannian scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$ is best understood 
780	as the construction of a quotient
781	$GL_{d,\Z}\backslash V$, where $V$ is the scheme of all
782	$d\times r$ matrices of rank $d$, and the group-scheme
783	$GL_{d,\Z}$ acts on $V$ on the left by matrix multiplication. However,
784	we will not use the language of group-scheme actions 
785	here, instead, we give a direct elementary construction of 
786	the Grassmannian scheme.
787	
788	The reader can take $d=1$ in what follows, in a first reading, to
789	get the special case $\grass(r,1) = \PP^{r-1}_{\Z}$, which 
790	has another construction as $\proj \Z[x_1,\ldots,x_r]$. 
791	
792	
793	
794	\medskip
795	
796	
797	
798	\textbf{Construction by gluing together affine patches }
799	For any integers $r\ge d\ge 1$, the Grassmannian scheme
800	$\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$, together with the tautological 
801	quotient $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ where \
802	$\U$ is a rank $d$ locally free sheaf on $\grass(r,d)$, 
803	can be explicitly constructed as follows.
804	
805	
806	
807	If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and 
808	$I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal 
809	to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will 
810	mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
811	$I$. 
812	
813	
814	For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$, 
815	consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
816	$X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
817	entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$. 
818	Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
819	$x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically 
820	isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$. 
821	
822	For any $J\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(J) =d$, let 
823	$P^I_J = \det(X^I_J) \in \Z[X^I]$
824	where $X^I_J$ is the $J$ th minor of $X^I$.
825	Let $U^I_J = \spec \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$
826	the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible. This means
827	the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
828	on $U^I_J$. 
829	
830	For any $I$ and $J$, a ring homomorphism 
831	$\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
832	defined as follows. The images of the variables 
833	$x^J_{p,q}$ are given by the entries of the matrix formula
834	$\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J) ^{-1}\,X^I$.
835	In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
836	so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$.
837	
838	Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and 
839	we leave it to the reader to verify that 
840	for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of 
841	cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition 
842	$\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
843	is satisfied. Therefore the schemes 
844	$U^I$, as $I$ varies over all the 
845	${r\choose d}$ different 
846	subsets of  $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of cardinality $d$,
847	can be glued together by the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$ to form a
848	finite-type scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$. As each $U^I$ is
849	isomorphic to $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$, it follows that
850	$\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is smooth of relative dimension $d(r-d)$.
851	
852	
853	
854	\medskip
855	
856	\textbf{Separatedness }
857	The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with $U^I\times U^J$
858	can be seen to be the closed subscheme $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$ 
859	defined by entries of the matrix formula $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$, 
860	and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme.
861	 
862	
863	\medskip
864	
865	\textbf{Properness }
866	We now show that $\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper. It is enough to
867	verify the valuative criterion of properness for discrete valuation rings.
868	Let $\Rr$ be a dvr, $\K$ its quotient field, and let
869	$\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$ be a morphism. This is given by 
870	a ring homomorphism $f : \Z[X^I] \to \K$ for some $I$. Having fixed
871	one such $I$, next choose $J$ such that $\nu(f(P^I_J))$ is minimum,
872	where $\nu : \K \to \Z\bigcup \{ \infty\}$ denotes 
873	the discrete valuation. As $P^I_I = 1$, note that
874	$\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0$, therefore
875	$f(P^I_J) \ne 0$ in $\K$ and so the matrix $f(X^I_J)$
876	lies in $GL_d(\K)$.
877	
878	Now consider the homomorphism
879	$g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
880	$$g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1}\,X^I)$$
881	Then $g$ defines the same morphism $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$,
882	and moreover all $d\times d$ minors $X^J_K$ satisfy
883	$\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$. 
884	As the minor $X^J_J$ is identity, it follows from the above that in fact
885	$\nu(g(x^J_{p,q}))\ge 0$ for all entries of $X^J$. 
886	Therefore, the map $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$
887	factors uniquely via $\Rr \subset \K$. The resulting morphism of
888	schemes $\spec \Rr \to U^J \hra \grass(r,d)$ prolongs
889	$\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$. We have already checked
890	separatedness of $\grass(r,d)$, so now we see that 
891	$\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper. 
892	
893	
894	\medskip
895	
896	
897	
898	\textbf{Universal quotient }
899	We next define a rank $d$ locally free sheaf $\U$ on $\grass(r,d)$
900	together with a surjective homomorphism $\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
901	On each $U^I$ we define a surjective homomorphism 
902	$u^I :\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I} \to  \oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
903	by the matrix $X^I$. Compatible with the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$
904	for gluing the affine pieces $U^I$, we give gluing data
905	$(g_{I,J})$ for gluing together the trivial bundles $\oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
906	by putting 
907	$$g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I_J)$$
908	This is compatible with the homomorphisms $u^I$, 
909	so we get a surjective homomorphism 
910	$u : \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
911	
912	
913	\medskip
914	\textbf{Projective embedding }
915	As $\U$ is given by the transition functions $g_{I,J}$ described above, 
916	the determinant line bundle 
917	$\det(\U)$ is given by the transition functions
918	$\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J \in GL_1(U^I_J)$.
919	For each $I$, we define a global section 
920	$$\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\grass(r,d),\det(\U))$$ 
921	by putting $\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I \in 
922	\Gamma(U^J,{\mathcal O}_{U^J})$ in terms of the trivialization over the open cover
923	$(U^J)$. 
924	We leave it to the reader to verify that the sections $\sigma_I$ form
925	a linear system which is base point free and separates points 
926	relative to $\spec \Z$, and so gives an embedding of 
927	$\grass(r,d)$ into $\PP^m_{\Z}$ where $m = {r\choose d}-1$.
928	This is a closed embedding by the properness of 
929	$\pi:\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$. In particular, $\det(\U)$
930	is a relatively very ample line bundle on $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$.

2154	\section{Construction of Quot Schemes}
2155	
2156	\medskip
2157	
2158	
2159	
2160	\centerline{\large\bf Notions of Projectivity}
2161	
2162	\medskip
2163	
2164	
2165	Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme. Recall that as defined by Grothendieck, 
2166	a morphism $X\to S$ is called a \textbf{projective morphism}
2167	if there exists a coherent sheaf $E$ on $S$, together with a 
2168	closed embedding of $X$ into $\PP(E) = \Proj \sym_{{\mathcal O}_S} E $ 
2169	over $S$. Equivalently, $X\to S$ is projective when 
2170	it is proper and there exists a relatively very ample
2171	line bundle $L$ on $X$ over $S$. These conditions are related by
2172	taking $L$ to be the restriction of ${\mathcal O}_{\PP(E)}(1)$ to $X$, or
2173	in the reverse direction, taking $E$ to be the direct image of $L$ on $S$.
2174	A morphism $X\to S$ is called \textbf{quasi-projective} if it factors as 
2175	an open embedding $X\hra Y$ followed by a projective morphism $Y \to S$.
2176	
2177	
2178	A stronger version of projectivity was introduced 
2179	by Altman and Kleiman: a morphism
2180	$X\to S$ of noetherian schemes is called \textbf{strongly projective} 
2181	(respectively, \textbf{strongly quasi-projective}) if
2182	there exists a vector bundle $E$ on $S$ together with a closed
2183	embedding (respectively, a locally closed embedding)
2184	$X\subset \PP(E)$ over $S$. 
2185	
2186	
2187	Finally, the strongest version of (quasi-)projectivity 
2188	is as follows (used for example in
2189	the textbook [H] by Hartshorne): a morphism
2190	$X\to S$ of noetherian schemes is projective in the strongest sense 
2191	if $X$ admits a (locally-)closed embedding into $\PP^n_S$ for some $n$.
2192	
2193	Note that none of the three versions of projectivity
2194	is local over the base $S$.
2195	
2196	\medskip
2197	
2198	\textbf{Exercises } (i) Gives examples to show that the above
2199	three notions of projectivity are in general distinct. 
2200	
2201	(ii) Show that if $X\to S$ is projective and flat, where $S$ is noetherian,
2202	then $X\to S$ is strongly projective.
2203	
2204	(iii) Note that if every coherent sheaf of ${\mathcal O}_S$-modules
2205	is the quotient of a vector bundle, then projectivity over the base $S$ 
2206	is equivalent to strong projectivity. If $S$ admits an ample line bundle 
2207	(for example, if $S$ is quasi-projective over an affine base), 
2208	then all three notions of projectivity over $S$ 
2209	are equivalent to each other.

2220	\centerline{\large\bf Main Existence Theorems}
2221	
2222	
2223	\medskip
2224	
2225	
2226	
2227	Grothendieck's original theorem on Quot schemes, 
2228	whose proof is outlined in [FGA] TDTE-IV, is the following.
2229	
2230	\begin{theorem}\label{Grothendieck Quot} {\rm (Grothendieck)}  
2231	Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme, $\pi: X\to S$ a projective morphism,
2232	and $L$ a relatively very ample line bundle on $X$. Then for any 
2233	coherent ${\mathcal O}_X$-module $E$ and any polynomial $\Phi \in \Q[\lambda]$,
2234	the functor $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ is representable
2235	by a projective $S$-scheme $\quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$.
2236	\end{theorem}
2237	
2238	
2239	Altman and Kleiman gave a complete and detailed proof of the existence of
2240	Quot schemes in [A-K 2]. They could remove the noetherian hypothesis, 
2241	by instead assuming strong (quasi-)projectivity of $X\to S$ 
2242	together with an assumption about the nature of the coherent  
2243	sheaf $E$, and deduce that the scheme $\quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ is then
2244	strongly (quasi-)projective over $S$.
2245	
2246	For simplicity, in these lecture notes we state and prove the result in [A-K 2]
2247	in the noetherian context.
2248	
2249	\begin{theorem}\label{Altman-Kleiman Quot} {\rm (Altman-Kleiman)}
2250	Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme, $X$ a closed subscheme of 
2251	$\PP(V)$ for some vector bundle $V$ on $S$, $L = {\mathcal O}_{\PP(V)}(1)|_X$,
2252	$E$ a coherent quotient sheaf of $\pi^*(W)(\nu)$ where
2253	$W$ is a vector bundle on $S$ and $\nu$ is an integer, and 
2254	$\Phi \in \Q[\lambda]$. 
2255	Then the functor $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ is representable
2256	by a scheme $\quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ which can be embedded over $S$ as a 
2257	closed subscheme of $\PP(F)$ for some vector bundle $F$ on $S$.
2258	
2259	The vector bundle $F$ can be chosen to be an exterior power of 
2260	the tensor product of $W$ with a symmetric powers of $V$.
2261	\end{theorem}
2262	
2263	Taking both $V$ and $W$ to be trivial in the above, we get the following.
2264	
2265	\begin{theorem}
2266	If $S$ is a noetherian scheme, $X$ is a closed subscheme of 
2267	$\PP^n_S$ for some $n\ge 0$, $L = {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n_S}(1)|_X$,
2268	$E$ is a coherent quotient sheaf of $\oplus^p {\mathcal O}_X(\nu)$ 
2269	for some integers $p\ge 0$ and $\nu$, and $\Phi \in \Q[\lambda]$,
2270	then the the functor $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ is representable
2271	by a scheme $\quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ which can be embedded over $S$ as a 
2272	closed subscheme of $\PP^r_S$ for some $r\ge 0$.
2273	\end{theorem}
2274	
2275	
2276	The rest of this section is devoted to proving 
2277	Theorem \ref{Altman-Kleiman Quot}, with extra noetherian hypothesis.
2278	At the end, we will remark on how the proof also
2279	gives us the original version of Grothendieck.
2280	
2281	
2282	
2283	
2284	
2285	
2286	\bigskip
2287	
2288	\centerline{\large\bf Reduction to the case of 
2289	$\Quot_{\pi^*W/\PP(V)/S}^{\Phi,L}$}
2290	
2291	\medskip
2292	
2293	
2294	It is enough to prove Theorem \ref{Altman-Kleiman Quot} in the special case
2295	that $X = \PP(V)$ and
2296	$E= \pi^*(W)$ where $V$ and $W$ are vector bundles on $S$, as
2297	a consequence of the next lemma.
2298	
2299	\begin{lemma}
2300	(i) Let $\nu$ be any integer. 
2301	Then tensoring by $L^{\nu}$ gives an isomorphism of
2302	functors from $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ to 
2303	$\Quot_{E(\nu)/X/S}^{\Psi,L}$ where the polynomial 
2304	$\Psi \in \Q[\lambda]$ is defined by $\Psi(\lambda) = \Phi(\lambda + \nu)$. 
2305	
2306	(ii) Let $\phi : E \to G$ be a surjective homomorphism of 
2307	coherent sheaves on $X$. Then the corresponding natural transformation
2308	$\Quot_{G/X/S}^{\Phi,L} \to \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$
2309	is a closed embedding.
2310	\end{lemma}
2311	
2312	\proof The statement (i) is obvious. The statement (ii) just says that
2313	given any locally noetherian scheme $T$ and a family
2314	$\langle \F,q\rangle \in \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(T)$, there exists
2315	a closed subscheme $T' \subset T$ with the following universal property:
2316	for any locally noetherian scheme $U$ and a morphism $f: U\to T$,
2317	the pulled back homomorphism of ${\mathcal O}_{X_U}$-modules $q_U : E_U \to \F_U$
2318	factors via the pulled back homomorphism $\phi_U : E_U \to G_U$
2319	if and only if $U \to T$ factors via $T'\hra T$. 
2320	This is satisfied by taking $T'$ to be the vanishing scheme
2321	for the composite homomorphism $\ker(\phi) \hra  E \stackrel{q}{\to} \F$
2322	of coherent sheaves on $X_T$ (see Remark \ref{vanishing scheme}), 
2323	which makes sense here as both $\ker(\phi)$ and $\F$ are coherent 
2324	on $X_T$ and $\F$ is flat over $T$. \qed
2325	
2326	
2327	
2328	Therefore if $\Quot_{\pi^*W/\PP(V)/S}^{\Phi,L}$ is
2329	representable, then for any coherent quotient
2330	$E$ of $\pi^*W(\nu)|_X$, we can take $\quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ 
2331	to be a closed subscheme of $\quot_{\pi^*W/\PP(V)/S}^{\Phi,L}$.
2332	
2333	
2334	
2335	
2336	
2337	
2338	
2339	
2340

2345	
2346	\centerline{\large\bf Use of $m$-Regularity}
2347	
2348	\medskip
2349	
2350	We consider the sheaf $E = \pi^*(W)$ on $X=\PP(V)$ where
2351	$V$ is a vector bundle on $S$,
2352	and take $L = {\mathcal O}_{\PP(V)}(1)$. 
2353	For any field $k$ and a $k$-valued point 
2354	$s$ of $S$, we have an isomorphism 
2355	$\PP(V)_s \simeq \PP^n_k$ where $n=\rank(V)-1$,
2356	and the restricted sheaf $E_s$ on $\PP(V)_s$ is isomorphic to 
2357	$\oplus^p{\mathcal O}_{\PP(V)_s}$ where $p=\rank(W)$. 
2358	It follows from Theorem \ref{Mumford}
2359	that given any $\Phi \in \Q[\lambda]$, there exists an integer
2360	$m$ which depends only on $\rank(V)$, $\rank(W)$ and $\Phi$,
2361	such that for any field $k$ and a $k$-valued point $s$ of $S$,
2362	the sheaf $E_s$ on $\PP(V)_s$ is $m$-regular, and for any  
2363	coherent quotient $q: E_s \to \F$ on $\PP(V)_s$ with 
2364	Hilbert polynomial $\Phi$, the sheaf $\F$ and the kernel sheaf
2365	$\G\subset E_s$ of $q$ are both $m$-regular. 
2366	In particular, it follows from the Castelnuovo Lemma \ref{Castelnuovo}
2367	that for $r\ge m$, all cohomologies 
2368	$H^i(X_s,E_s(r))$, $H^i(X_s,\F(r))$, and $H^i(X_s,\G(r))$ 
2369	are zero for $i\ge 1$,
2370	and $H^0(X_s,E_s(r))$, $H^0(X_s,\F(r))$, and $H^0(X_s,\G(r))$ 
2371	are generated by their global sections.
2372	
2373	From the above it follows by Theorem \ref{Base-change for flat sheaves}
2374	that if $T$ is an $S$-scheme and
2375	$q :E_T \to \F$ is a $T$-flat coherent quotient with Hilbert polynomial
2376	$\Phi$, then we have the following, where $\G\subset E_T$ is
2377	the kernel of $q$.
2378	
2379	\textbf{(*) } The sheaves ${\pi_T}_*\G(r)$, ${\pi_T}_*E_T(r)$, 
2380	${\pi_T}_*\F(r)$ are locally free of fixed ranks 
2381	determined by the data $n$, $p$, $r$, and $\Phi$, 
2382	the homomorphisms ${\pi_T}^*{\pi_T}_*(\G(r))\to \G(r)$, 
2383	${\pi_T}^*{\pi_T}_*(E_T(r))\to E_T(r)$, 
2384	${\pi_T}^*{\pi_T}_*(\F(r))\to \F(r)$ are 
2385	surjective, and the higher direct images  
2386	$R^i{\pi_T}_*\G(r)$, $R^i{\pi_T}_*E_T(r)$, 
2387	$R^i{\pi_T}_*\F(r)$ are zero, for all $r\ge m$ and $i\ge 1$. 
2388	
2389	\textbf{(**) }
2390	In particular we have the following commutative diagram
2391	of locally sheaves on $X_T$, in which both rows are exact, and all 
2392	three vertical maps are surjective.
2393	
2394	%{\footnotesize
2395	$$\begin{array}{ccccccc}
2396	0  \to & {\pi_T}^*{\pi_T}_*(\G(r)) & \to 
2397	& {\pi_T}^*{\pi_T}_* (E_T(r)) &
2398	\to  &{\pi_T}^*{\pi_T}_* (\F(r))& \to   0\\ 
2399	       & \dna                &     & \dna                &
2400	     & \dna               &        \\
2401	0  \to & \G(r)                  & \to & E(r) &
2402	\to  & \F(r)                 & \to   0
2403	\end{array}$$
2404	%}% end of footnotesize
2405	
2406	\bigskip
2407	
2408	
2409	\centerline{\large\bf Embedding Quot into Grassmannian}
2410	
2411	\medskip
2412	
2413	We now fix a positive integer $r$ such that $r\ge m$.
2414	Note that the rank of ${\pi_T}_*\F(r)$ is $\Phi(r)$
2415	and $\pi_*E(r) = W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V$.
2416	Therefore the surjective homomorphism
2417	${\pi_T}_*E_T(r) \to {\pi_T}_*\F(r)$
2418	defines an element of the set 
2419	$\Grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V , \Phi(r))(T)$.
2420	We thus get a morphism of functors 
2421	$$\alpha : \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \to 
2422	\Grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V, \Phi(r))$$
2423	It associates to $q: E_T \to \F$ the quotient
2424	${\pi_T}_*(q(r)) : {\pi_T}_*E_T(r) \to {\pi_T}_*\F(r)$.
2425	 
2426	The above morphism $\alpha$ is injective because the quotient 
2427	$q: E_T \to \F$ can be recovered from 
2428	${\pi_T}_*(q(r)) : {\pi_T}_*E_T(r) \to {\pi_T}_*\F(r)$ 
2429	as follows. 
2430	
2431	If $G = \grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V, \Phi(r))$ with
2432	projection $p_G : G \to S$, and
2433	$u : {p_G}^*E \to \U$ denotes the universal quotient on $G$
2434	with kernel $v : \K \to {p_G}^*E$,
2435	then the homomorphism 
2436	${\pi_T}^*{\pi_T}_*(\G(r))\to {\pi_T}^*{\pi_T}_*E_T(r)$ 
2437	can be recovered 
2438	from the morphism $T \to G$ as
2439	the pull-back of $v : \K \to {p_G}^*E$.
2440	Let $h$ be  the composite 
2441	${\pi_T}^*{\pi_T}_*(\G(r))\to {\pi_T}^*{\pi_T}_*(E_T(r))\to E_T(r)$.
2442	As a consequence of the properties of the diagram \textbf{(**)}, 
2443	the following is a right exact sequence on $X_T$
2444	$${\pi_T}^*{\pi_T}_*(\G(r))\stackrel{h}{\to} 
2445	E_T(r) \stackrel{q(r)}{\to} \F \to 0$$
2446	and so $q(r) : E_T(r) \to \F(r)$ can be recovered as the cokernel of $h$.
2447	Finally, twisting by $-r$, we recover $q$, proving the desired 
2448	injectivity of the morphism of functors 
2449	$\alpha :\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \to 
2450	\Grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V, \Phi(r))$.
2451	
2452	
2453	
2454	\bigskip
2455	
2456	\centerline{\large\bf Use of Flattening Stratification}
2457	\nopagebreak
2458	\medskip
2459	\nopagebreak
2460	We will next prove that 
2461	$\alpha :\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \to 
2462	\Grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V, \Phi(r))$
2463	is relatively representable. In fact, we will show that
2464	given any locally noetherian $S$-scheme $T$ and
2465	a surjective homomorphism $f :  W_T \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_T}\sym^r V_T \to \J$
2466	where $\J$ is a locally free ${\mathcal O}_T$-module of rank $\Phi(r)$,
2467	there exists a locally closed subscheme $T'$ of 
2468	$T$ with the following universal property \textbf{(F)} : 
2469	
2470	\textbf{(F) } Given any 
2471	locally noetherian $S$-scheme $Y$ and an $S$-morphism $\phi :Y\to T$,
2472	let $f_Y$ be the pull-back of $f$, and  
2473	let $\K_Y = \ker(f_Y) = \phi^* \ker(f)$. Let
2474	$\pi_Y : X_Y \to Y$ be the projection, and let
2475	$h : {\pi_Y}^* \K_Y \to E_Y$ be the composite map
2476	$${\pi_Y}^* \K_Y \to  {\pi_Y}^* (W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V)
2477	= {\pi_Y}^*{\pi_Y}_*E_Y \to  E_Y$$ 
2478	Let $q:   E_Y \to \F$ be the cokernel of $h$. 
2479	Then $\F$ is flat over $Y$ with its Hilbert polynomial on all fibers
2480	equal to $\Phi$ if and only if $\phi :Y\to T$
2481	factors via $Y'\hra Y$.
2482	
2483	The existence of such a locally closed subscheme $T'$ of 
2484	$T$ is given by Theorem \ref{flattening stratification}, which 
2485	shows that $T'$ is the stratum corresponding to 
2486	Hilbert polynomial $\Phi$ for the 
2487	flattening stratification over $T$ for the sheaf $\F$ on $X_T$.
2488	
2489	When we take $T$ to be $\grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V, \Phi(r))$ 
2490	with universal quotient $u : {p_G}^*E \to \U$, the corresponding
2491	locally closed subscheme $T'$ represents
2492	the functor $\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}$ by its construction.
2493	
2494	Hence we have shown that $\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}$ is represented
2495	by a locally closed subscheme of 
2496	$\grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V, \Phi(r))$. As 
2497	$\grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V, \Phi(r))$ embeds as a 
2498	closed subscheme of $\PP(\bigwedge ^{\Phi(r)} W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V)$,
2499	we get a locally closed embedding of $S$-schemes 
2500	$$\quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \subset 
2501	\PP(\bigwedge ^{\Phi(r)} (W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V))$$
2502	In particular, the morphism $\quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\to S$ 
2503	is separated and of finite type.
2504	
2505	
2506	
2507	\bigskip
2508	
2509	
2510	\centerline{\large\bf Valuative Criterion for Properness}
2511	
2512	
2513	\medskip
2514	
2515	The original reference for the following argument is EGA IV (2) 2.8.1. 
2516	
2517	The functor $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ satisfies the following valuative
2518	criterion for properness over $S$: given any discrete valuation ring
2519	$R$ over $S$ with quotient field $K$, the restriction map
2520	$$\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec R) \to \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec K)$$
2521	is bijective. This can be seen as follows.
2522	Given any coherent quotient 
2523	$q: E_K \to \F$ on $X_R$ which defines an element $\langle \F, q\rangle$
2524	of $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec K)$.
2525	Let $\ov{\F}$ be the image of the composite homomorphism
2526	$E_R \to j_*(E_K) \to j_*\F$ where $j : X_K \hra X_R$ is the open
2527	inclusion. Let $\ov{q} : E_R\to \ov{\F}$ be the induced surjection.
2528	Then we leave it to the reader to verify that 
2529	$\langle \F, q\rangle$ is an element of $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec R)$
2530	which maps to $\langle \F, q\rangle$, and is the unique such element.
2531	(Use the basic fact that being flat over a dvr 
2532	is the same as being torsion-free.)
2533	
2534	As $S$ is noetherian and as we have already shown that 
2535	$\quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\to S$ is of finite type,
2536	it follows that $\quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\to S$ is a proper morphism.
2537	Therefore the embedding of
2538	$\quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}$ into 
2539	$\PP(\bigwedge ^{\Phi(r)} (W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V))$
2540	is a closed embedding.
2541	
2542	This completes the proof of Theorem \ref{Altman-Kleiman Quot}.\hfill$\square$
2543	
2544	
2545	
2546	
2547	
2548	\bigskip
2549	
2550	
2551	\centerline{\large\bf The Version of Grothendieck}
2552	
2553	
2554	\medskip
2555	
2556	
2557	We now describe how to get Theorem \ref{Grothendieck Quot}
2558	from the above proof. As $S$ is noetherian, we can find a common
2559	$m$ such that given any field-valued point 
2560	$s: \spec k\to S$ and a coherent quotient $q: E_s \to \F$ on $X_s$
2561	with Hilbert polynomial $\Phi$,
2562	the sheaves $E_s(r)$, $\F(r)$, $\G(r)$ (where $\G = \ker(q)$) are
2563	generated by global sections and all their higher cohomologies vanish,
2564	whenever $r\ge m$.
2565	This follows from the theory of $m$-regularity, and semi-continuity. 
2566	
2567	Because we have such a common $m$, we get as before an injective morphism
2568	from the functor $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ into the Grassmannian functor
2569	$\Grass(\pi_*E(r), \Phi(r))$. The sheaf 
2570	$\pi_*E(r)$ is coherent, but need not be the quotient of a 
2571	vector bundle on $S$. Consequently, the scheme $\grass(\pi_*E(r), \Phi(r))$
2572	is projective over the base, but not necessarily strongly projective.
2573	
2574	Finally, the use of flattening stratification, which can be 
2575	made over an affine open cover of $S$, gives 
2576	a locally closed subscheme of $\grass(\pi_*E(r), \Phi(r))$ which represents
2577	$\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$, which is in fact a
2578	closed subscheme by the valuative criterion. 
2579	Thus, we get $\quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ as a projective scheme over $S$.

947	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change]
948	\label{lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology}
949	Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
950	$$
951	\xymatrix{
952	X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\
953	S' \ar[r]^g & S
954	}
955	$$
956	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
957	with pullback $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$.
958	Assume that $g$ is flat and that $f$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.
959	For any $i \geq 0$
960	\begin{enumerate}
961	\item the base change map of
962	Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-base-change-map-flat-case}
963	is an isomorphism
964	$$
965	g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
966	$$
967	\item if $S = \Spec(A)$ and $S' = \Spec(B)$, then
968	$H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
969	\end{enumerate}
970	\end{lemma}
971	
972	\begin{proof}
973	Using Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-base-change-map-flat-case} in (1)
974	is allowed since $g'$ is flat by
975	Morphisms, Lemma \ref{morphisms-lemma-base-change-flat}.
976	Having said this, part (1) follows from part (2). Namely,
977	part (1) is local on $S'$ and hence we may assume $S$
978	and $S'$ are affine. In other words, we have $S = \Spec(A)$
979	and $S' = \Spec(B)$ as in (2).

\chapter{The Quot scheme}
\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean

% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.2.b sub-row of Route~A
% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
% $\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}$ -- a projective $S$-scheme representing the
% functor of $T$-flat coherent quotients of $E_T$ on $X_T = X \times_S T$ with
% Hilbert polynomial $\Phi$ on every fiber. The Quot construction is the
% \emph{existence engine} behind the relative Picard functor: A.2.c
% (\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) wires this chapter together with
% the relative Picard functor (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}, A.1.c)
% through the Abel map $\Div_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$, which factors
% through the Hilbert scheme $\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}$ -- the
% case $E = \mathcal{O}_X$ of the Quot functor. This chapter is gated on
% A.2.a (\cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}, written this iter) and
% A.1.a (\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}, on disk).
%
% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The construction embeds Quot as a
% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
% $\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))$.
% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability; it is a
% candidate for promotion to its own chapter
% \texttt{Picard\_GrassmannianScheme.tex} (writer iter-175+) -- see "Notes
% for Plan Agent" in the writer report.

\section{Setup and motivation}
\label{sec:quot_setup}

Throughout this chapter, let $S$ be a noetherian scheme, $\pi : X \to S$ a
projective morphism, $L$ a relatively very ample line bundle on $X$ over $S$,
and $E$ a coherent sheaf on $X$. The Quot construction packages the
$T$-flat coherent quotients $E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}$ of the
pull-back $E_T$ to $X_T = X \times_S T$, modulo the equivalence
$\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle$ defined by
$\ker(q) = \ker(q')$. Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
$\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]$
(constant on connected $T$ by flatness; see~\S\ref{sec:hilbert_polynomial})
stratifies the Quot functor into pieces $\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}$, each of
which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective $S$-scheme.

% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1 and
% \S 5; FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500 +
% L2154-L2542). The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
% the verbatim source-quote in \S\ref{sec:hilbert_polynomial} is taken from
% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
% reference).

\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}

\begin{definition}[Hilbert polynomial]
  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
  % Polynomials" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
  %  a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
  %  Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
  %  the function
  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that $\chi(F(m))$
  %  is indeed a polynomial in $m$ under the above assumption is a special
  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
  %
  %  Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
  %  be any coherent sheaf on
  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$.
  %  If $\F$ is flat over $S$ then the function $s\mapsto {\Phi}_s$
  %  from the set of points of $S$ to the polynomial ring $\Q[\lambda]$ is
  %  known to be locally constant on $S$."
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
  III.5.2.}
  Let $X \to S$ be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let $L$
  be a line bundle on $X$. For a coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ on $X$ whose
  schematic support is proper over $S$ and a point $s \in S$, the
  \emph{Hilbert polynomial of $\mathcal{F}$ at $s$ relative to $L$} is the
  polynomial $\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]$ characterised by
  \[
    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}|_{X_s} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}|_{X_s} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
  \]
  (a polynomial in $m$ by Snapper's Lemma; the sum is finite by coherence and
  properness). When $\mathcal{F}$ is $S$-flat the function
  $s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}$ is locally constant on $S$, so $\Phi$ is
  well-defined on connected components.
\end{definition}

\section{The Quot functor}
\label{sec:quot_functor}

\begin{definition}[Quot functor]
  \label{def:quot_functor}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor}
  \uses{def:hilbert_polynomial}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors $\Hilb_{X/S}$ and
  % $\Quot_{E/X/S}$" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L394-L433

  %  over $S$. For any $T\to S$ in $Sch_S$, a \textbf{family of quotients of $E$
